pattern: Every fixed-length window has its own 'x-sum,' the sum of the highest-frequency values it contains, and recomputing that sum window by window is enough once the array is small.
hints: Think about what information you need per window: how often each value occurs, and which values currently rank in the top x by that count.
Recomputing frequencies from scratch for every window is enough when the array is small -- group each window's values, sort by count then value, and add up the top x.
Once you trust the brute recomputation, the same idea generalizes to a sliding window: only two values change frequency when the window shifts by one, which is the key to speeding it up.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, mixed frequencies with a tie broken by value
statement example, k equals x so every value is kept
k = 1, x = 1: each window is a single element
k equals the array length: only one window exists
counts tie so the bigger value must win, not the first seen
fewer distinct values than x: the x-sum is just the whole window
all distinct values, count is always 1 so bigger values are kept first

stress.description: random small arrays with random valid k and x
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 50))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    x = rng.randint(1, k)
    return [nums, k, x]
